title: ISCO-08 Standardization and Validation
text:
Code
# clear global env and force full garbage collection
rm(list = ls(envir = .GlobalEnv), envir = .GlobalEnv)
invisible(gc(full = TRUE))

# detach non-base packages to avoid namespace interference
invisible(lapply(grep(":|autoloads|globalenv", search(),
  invert = TRUE, value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE
), detach, character.only = TRUE, unload = TRUE, force = TRUE))

# source custom functions for text processing and data handling
purrr::walk(file.path(here::here("assets", "functions"), paste0(c(
  "sci_to_tex", "md_to_html", "set_globals", "decapitalize",
  "save_function", "add_style", "suppress_output", "import_pipes",
  "numeric_df", "as_kable", "empty_to_na", "drop_all_na_rows",
  "named_list", "std_text", "beautify_names", "footnote"
), ".R")), source)

# set global options and import pipe operators
set_globals()
import_pipes()

title: ISCO-08 Standardization and Validation
text:
1 Define Constants
Respondent-type suffixes govern column naming throughout the step.


Code
# suffixes map respondent roles to column name patterns
# empty string for respondent self, underscore-prefixed for others
suffixes <- c("partner", "mother", "father") %>%
  setNames(object = sprintf("_%s", .)) %>%
  c("respondent" = "", .)

# occupation column names derived from suffixes
occupation_columns <- paste0("occupation", unname(suffixes))

title: ISCO-08 Standardization and Validation
text:
2 Load Data


Code
# load initial dataset and rename ISCO-08 columns for clarity
main <- haven::read_sav(here::here("assets", "data", "main_initial.sav")) %>%
  dplyr::rename(
    isco08_old = BRC14,
    isco08_old_father = BRC14_father,
    isco08_old_mother = BRC14_mother,
    isco08_old_partner = BRC14_partner
  )

# import corrected ISCO-08 codes from external source
xlsx <- readxl::read_xlsx(
  here::here("assets", "data", "isco08_codes.xlsx"),
  sheet = 1
)

title: ISCO-08 Standardization and Validation
text:
3 Clean and Standardize Data

in: main
out: main_with_copies with _init and _cleaned occupation columns
rule: set two invalid entries (idnummer 2023, 1854) to NA; standardize text via std_text()



Code
# replace invalid occupation entries with NA (case-specific corrections)
main[main$idnummer == 2023, "occupation_mother"] <- NA
main[main$idnummer == 1854, "occupation_partner"] <- NA

# generate initial and cleaned copies of occupation data
main_with_copies <- main %>%
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(
    tidyselect::all_of(occupation_columns),
    list(
      init = ~.,
      cleaned = ~ std_text(.)
    ),
    .names = "{col}_{fn}"
  ))

title: ISCO-08 Standardization and Validation
ISCO-08 Standardization and Validation
text:
5 Preserve Mapping Functions


Code
# suppress output during function saving
suppress_output({
  save_function(brc14_map)
  save_function(isco08_to_brc14)
  save_function(isco08_to_isei08)
})

title: ISCO-08 Standardization and Validation
text:
6 Integrate Corrected ISCO Codes


Code
# standardize column names for merging across respondent types
rename_and_select <- function(data, suffix = "") {
  require(rlang, quietly = TRUE)
  suffix <- if (suffix == "") suffix else paste0("_", suffix)

  out <- data %>%
    dplyr::rename(
      !!paste0("isco08_old", suffix) := isco08_init,
      !!paste0("occupation", if (suffix == "") "" else suffix, "_cleaned") := occupation,
      !!paste0("isco08", suffix) := isco08,
      !!paste0("isco08_new", suffix) := isco08_new
    ) %>%
    dplyr::select(-source)

  names(out) <- gsub("[_]+", "_", names(out))
  return(out)
}



in: xlsx, main_with_copies
out: groups_with_classifications (with BRC14 and ISEI-08 columns)
rule: justified corrections (isco08_new) coalesce over the curator’s final codes (isco08); initial codes the curator nullified remain NA; first non-missing value per idnummer



Code
# select relevant columns from corrected data
corrected <- xlsx %>%
  dplyr::select(idnummer, source, occupation, isco08, isco08_init, isco08_new)

# curator code columns are stored as text cells in the workbook; parse to
# numeric and fail loudly if any non-empty value is not a clean code
parse_code <- function(x) {
  chr <- dplyr::na_if(trimws(as.character(x)), "")
  out <- suppressWarnings(as.numeric(chr))
  stopifnot(identical(is.na(out), is.na(chr)))
  out
}

corrected <- corrected %>%
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(c(isco08, isco08_init, isco08_new), parse_code))

# split corrected data by respondent type
groups <- dplyr::group_split(corrected, source)

# apply renaming and remove fully NA rows before merging
group_sources <- purrr::map_chr(groups, ~ as.character(.x$source[[1]]))
suffixes_ordered <- suffixes[group_sources]

groups <- setNames(
  object = purrr::map2(groups, unname(suffixes_ordered), rename_and_select),
  nm = unname(suffixes_ordered)
) %>%
  lapply(drop_all_na_rows)

# treat "" as missing, then take first non-missing value
pick_first_non_na <- function(x) {
  if (is.character(x)) x[x == ""] <- NA_character_
  y <- x[!is.na(x)]
  if (length(y) == 0) {
    return(x[1][NA]) # returns typed NA
  }
  y[[1]]
}

# quick diagnostics per group (which ids are duplicated)
dup_ids_by_group <- purrr::imap(
  groups,
  ~ .x %>%
    dplyr::count(idnummer, name = "n") %>%
    dplyr::filter(n > 1) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(source = .y) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(n), idnummer)
)

# collapse each group to one row per idnummer
groups_1row <- purrr::map(
  groups,
  ~ .x %>%
    dplyr::group_by(idnummer) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      dplyr::across(dplyr::everything(), pick_first_non_na),
      .groups = "drop"
    )
)

# flag ids where a column had >1 distinct non-missing value inside a group
conflicts_by_group <- purrr::imap(
  groups,
  ~ .x %>%
    dplyr::group_by(idnummer) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      dplyr::across(
        dplyr::everything(),
        ~ dplyr::n_distinct(.[!is.na(.)]) > 1
      ),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::filter(dplyr::if_any(-idnummer, isTRUE)) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(source = .y)
)

# join one-to-one to prevent many-to-many issues
groups <- groups_1row %>%
  Reduce(f = function(x, y) {
    dplyr::full_join(x, y, by = "idnummer", relationship = "one-to-one")
  })

# coalesce old and new ISCO codes for each respondent type
groups_coalesced <- groups %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    isco08r = dplyr::coalesce(isco08_new, isco08),
    isco08r_father = dplyr::coalesce(isco08_new_father, isco08_father),
    isco08r_mother = dplyr::coalesce(isco08_new_mother, isco08_mother),
    isco08r_partner = dplyr::coalesce(isco08_new_partner, isco08_partner)
  )

# generate BRC14 and ISEI-08 classifications from final ISCO codes
groups_with_classifications <- groups_coalesced %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(
      dplyr::matches("isco08r"),
      ~ isco08_to_brc14(.),
      .names = "{gsub('isco08r', 'brc14', .col)}"
    ),
    dplyr::across(
      dplyr::matches("isco08r"),
      ~ isco08_to_isei08(.),
      .names = "{gsub('isco08r', 'isei08', .col)}"
    )
  )

title: ISCO-08 Standardization and Validation
text:
7 Finalize Analytical Dataset

in: main_with_copies, groups_with_classifications
out: main_corrected.sav
rule: empty strings to NA; occupation text set to NA where final ISCO-08 code is NA



Code
# replace empty strings with NA in character columns
remove_empty_strings <- function(data_frame) {
  dplyr::mutate(
    data_frame,
    dplyr::across(
      dplyr::where(is.character),
      ~ dplyr::if_else(.x == "", NA_character_, .x)
    )
  )
}

# subset relevant columns and clean empty strings
groups_subset <- groups_with_classifications %>%
  dplyr::select(
    idnummer,
    dplyr::all_of(paste0(occupation_columns, "_cleaned")),
    dplyr::matches("isco08r"),
    dplyr::matches("brc14", ignore.case = FALSE),
    dplyr::matches("isei08")
  ) %>%
  remove_empty_strings()

# merge with main dataset and standardize column names
main_cleaned <- main_with_copies %>%
  remove_empty_strings() %>%
  dplyr::select(-dplyr::any_of(c("source", "imputed"))) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    groups_subset %>%
      dplyr::select(-dplyr::all_of(paste0(occupation_columns, "_cleaned"))),
    by = "idnummer",
    relationship = "one-to-one"
  ) %>%
  setNames(decapitalize(names(.)))

# guard against silent row inflation from the merge
stopifnot(nrow(main_cleaned) == nrow(main_with_copies))

# ensure occupation aligns with corrected ISCO codes
for (suffix in unname(suffixes)) {
  isco_col <- paste0("isco08r", suffix)
  occ_col <- paste0("occupation", suffix)
  main_cleaned[[occ_col]][is.na(main_cleaned[[isco_col]])] <- NA
}
main <- main_cleaned

# export corrected dataset for subsequent use
haven::write_sav(main, here::here("assets", "data", "saved", "main_corrected.sav"))

title: ISCO-08 Standardization and Validation
text:
8 Visualize Classification Corrections

Code
# scatter plot with regression line to visualize code corrections
scatter <- function(x_var, y_var, title, point_size = 2) {
  # filter complete cases for plotting
  df_clean <- main %>%
    dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(c(x_var, y_var))) %>%
    dplyr::filter(complete.cases(.))

  # compute linear model for trend visualization
  fit <- lm(as.formula(paste(y_var, "~", x_var)), data = df_clean)

  # add residuals and predictions to dataset
  df_clean$resids <- residuals(fit)
  df_clean$y_hat <- predict(fit)
  df_clean$resids_trans <- sqrt(abs(df_clean$resids))

  # generate scatter plot with color-coded deviations
  df_clean %>%
    ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(
      x = .data[[x_var]],
      y = .data[[y_var]],
      color = resids_trans
    )) +
    ggplot2::geom_smooth(
      method = "lm",
      se = FALSE,
      color = "black",
      linewidth = 0.8,
      formula = y ~ x
    ) +
    ggplot2::geom_jitter(
      size = point_size,
      alpha = 0.6,
      width = 0.2,
      height = 0.2
    ) +
    ggplot2::scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "Deviation") +
    ggplot2::labs(
      title = title,
      x = "ISCO-08 (Corrected)",
      y = "ISCO-08 (Original)"
    )
}

# plot comparisons for each respondent type
scatter("isco08r", "isco08_old", "Respondent")
scatter("isco08r_partner", "isco08_old_partner", "Partner")
scatter("isco08r_father", "isco08_old_father", "Father")
scatter("isco08r_mother", "isco08_old_mother", "Mother")




















Comparison of the Initial and Corrected ISCO-08 Codes

title: ISCO-08 Standardization and Validation
text:
9 Summarize ISCO Corrections
Changed digits appear in red and unchanged digits in purple within each correction table.


Code
# apply HTML formatting to column names (reduce bracket font size)
format_brackets <- function(vec) {
  stringr::str_replace_all(vec, c(
    "\\(([^)]+)\\)" = "<span style='font-size: 0.8em; white-space: nowrap;'>(\\1)</span>",
    "ISCO-08" = "ISCO‑08"
  ))
}

# column headers for ISCO corrections table
col_names <- format_brackets(c(
  "ID No.",
  "Occupation (as reported)$\\ ^1$",
  "ISCO-08 (original)",
  "ISCO-08 Group (original)",
  "ISCO-08 (corrected)",
  "ISCO-08 Group (corrected)",
  "Justification"
))




Code
# split strings into characters and pad for alignment
split_and_pad <- function(x, max_len) {
  chars <- unlist(strsplit(as.character(x), ""))
  pad <- rep("_", max_len - nchar(x))
  c(chars, pad)
}

# highlight differing digits in corrected codes
highlight_diff_digits <- function(a, b) {
  # return b if either input is NA
  if (anyNA(c(a, b))) {
    return(as.character(b))
  }

  # determine maximum length for padding
  max_len <- max(nchar(a), nchar(b))

  # split and pad strings to uniform length
  a_digs <- split_and_pad(a, max_len)
  b_digs <- split_and_pad(b, max_len)

  # define colors for unchanged and changed digits
  clr <- viridis::plasma(20, 1, 0)[seq(4, 12, length.out = 2)]

  # format digits with HTML color coding
  formatted <- paste0(
    mapply(function(init, new) {
      sprintf(
        '<span style="color:%s;">%s</span>',
        ifelse(init == new, clr[1], clr[2]),
        new
      )
    }, a_digs, b_digs),
    collapse = ""
  )

  # return b if formatting fails
  if (is.na(formatted)) {
    return(as.character(b))
  }
  return(formatted)
}




Code
# generate summary table of ISCO corrections with explicit source name parameter
summarize_isco_corrections <- function(x, source_name = NULL) {
  # use provided source name or infer from variable name
  source_name <- source_name %||% deparse(substitute(x))

  # calculate total rows and justified corrections
  tot_rows <- nrow(x)
  just_df <- dplyr::filter(x, !is.na(isco08_new))
  just_rows <- nrow(just_df)
  pct_just <- round(just_rows / tot_rows * 100)

  footnote_text <- glue(
    "Correction records: $N={just_rows}$ ({pct_just}% of the {tot_rows} cases in ",
    "{source_name}). Occupation titles remain in lowercase for consistency with ",
    "reported data. Revised ISCO-08 codes reflect updates from original ",
    "classifications, supported by a rationale for each change. Red digits ",
    "highlight code modifications, while unchanged digits appear in purple."
  )

  # format table with styling and footnote
  just_df %>%
    as_kable() %>%
    kableExtra::column_spec(seq(ncol(just_df)), extra_css = "vertical-align: top;") %>%
    footnote() %>%
    add_style()
}




Code
# process corrected data and split by respondent type
xlsx_grouped <- xlsx %>%
  dplyr::rowwise() %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    # apply highlighting to corrected ISCO codes
    isco08_new = highlight_diff_digits(isco08_init, isco08_new)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::ungroup() %>%
  # drop unnecessary column, if present
  dplyr::select(-dplyr::any_of("isco08")) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(source)

xlsx_split <- dplyr::group_split(xlsx_grouped, .keep = FALSE)
names(xlsx_split) <- dplyr::group_keys(xlsx_grouped)$source

# place split groups in the global environment for downstream chunks
list2env(xlsx_split, envir = .GlobalEnv)


<environment: R_GlobalEnv>




Code
# compute correction statistics per source with totals
named_list(respondent, partner, mother, father) %>%
  purrr::map_dfr(~ tibble::tibble(
    total = nrow(.x), corrected = sum(!is.na(.x$isco08_new))
  ), .id = "source") %>%
  dplyr::bind_rows(dplyr::summarise(., source = "Total", dplyr::across(where(is.integer), sum))) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(source = stringr::str_to_title(source), pct = round(corrected / total * 100, 1)) %>%
  dplyr::rename(Category = source, `Total Records` = total, Corrected = corrected, `%` = pct) %$%
  kableExtra::row_spec(kable_input = as_kable(.), nrow(.), bold = TRUE) %>%
  add_style()

title: Environment Setup & Dependencies
text:
1 Leveraged Packages
Auto-generated by scanning package::function calls across all analysis/ step files.


Code
pkg_regex <- "([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9.]*)::([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9._]*)\\s*\\((?!\\s*['\"])"
purrr::map(
  list.files(
    here::here("analysis"),
    full.names = TRUE,
    pattern = "index\\.qmd$",
    recursive = TRUE
  ),
  ~ readLines(.x) %>%
    stringr::str_match_all(pkg_regex) %>%
    purrr::map(~ .x[, 2])
) %>%
  unlist() %>%
  Filter(f = nchar) %>%
  table() %>%
  sort(decreasing = TRUE) %>%
  names() %>%
  purrr::map_dfr(~ {
    x <- utils::packageDescription(.x)
    url <- if (!is.null(x$URL) && is.character(x$URL)) {
      if (startsWith(x$URL, "doi:")) {
        paste0("https://doi.org/", sub("doi:", "", x$URL))
      } else {
        strsplit(x$URL, "\n|,")[[1]][1]
      }
    } else {
      sprintf("https://cran.r-project.org/package=%s", .x)
    }

    name <- x$Package %>%
      ifelse(test = is.null(url), sprintf("[%s](%s)", ., url)) %>%
      md_to_html()

    data.frame(
      Package = name,
      Description = md_to_html(stringr::str_squish(x$Description)),
      Version = x$Version,
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  }) %>%
  as_kable() %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(1, extra_css = "white-space: nowrap;") %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(1:3, extra_css = "vertical-align: top;") %>%
  add_style()




List of packages used in the analysis pipeline


Package
Description
Version




dplyr
A fast, consistent tool for working with data frame like objects, both in memory and out of memory.
1.2.1


ggplot2
A system for ‘declaratively’ creating graphics, based on “The Grammar of Graphics”. You provide the data, tell ‘ggplot2’ how to map variables to aesthetics, what graphical primitives to use, and it takes care of the details.
4.0.3


purrr
A complete and consistent functional programming toolkit for R.
1.2.2


stats
R statistical functions.
4.6.0


kableExtra
Build complex HTML or ‘LaTeX’ tables using ‘kable()’ from ‘knitr’ and the piping syntax from ‘magrittr’. Function ‘kable()’ is a light weight table generator coming from ‘knitr’. This package simplifies the way to manipulate the HTML or ‘LaTeX’ codes generated by ‘kable()’ and allows users to construct complex tables and customize styles using a readable syntax.
1.4.0


parallel
Support for parallel computation, including by forking (taken from package multicore), by sockets (taken from package snow) and random-number generation.
4.6.0


haven
Import foreign statistical formats into R via the embedded ‘ReadStat’ C library, https://github.com/WizardMac/ReadStat.
2.5.5


tibble
Provides a ‘tbl_df’ class (the ‘tibble’) with stricter checking and better formatting than the traditional data frame.
3.3.1


grid
A rewrite of the graphics layout capabilities, plus some support for interaction.
4.6.0


htmltools
Tools for HTML generation and output.
0.5.9


labelled
Work with labelled data imported from ‘SPSS’ or ‘Stata’ with ‘haven’ or ‘foreign’. This package provides useful functions to deal with “haven_labelled” and “haven_labelled_spss” classes introduced by ‘haven’ package.
2.16.0


lavaan
Fit a variety of latent variable models, including confirmatory factor analysis, structural equation modeling and latent growth curve models.
0.6-21


viridis
Color maps designed to improve graph readability for readers with common forms of color blindness and/or color vision deficiency. The color maps are also perceptually-uniform, both in regular form and also when converted to black-and-white for printing. This package also contains ‘ggplot2’ bindings for discrete and continuous color and fill scales. A lean version of the package called ‘viridisLite’ that does not include the ‘ggplot2’ bindings can be found at https://cran.r-project.org/package=viridisLite.
0.6.5


stringr
A consistent, simple and easy to use set of wrappers around the fantastic ‘stringi’ package. All function and argument names (and positions) are consistent, all functions deal with “NA”’s and zero length vectors in the same way, and the output from one function is easy to feed into the input of another.
1.6.0


tidyr
Tools to help to create tidy data, where each column is a variable, each row is an observation, and each cell contains a single value. ‘tidyr’ contains tools for changing the shape (pivoting) and hierarchy (nesting and ‘unnesting’) of a dataset, turning deeply nested lists into rectangular data frames (‘rectangling’), and extracting values out of string columns. It also includes tools for working with missing values (both implicit and explicit).
1.3.2


compiler
Byte code compiler for R.
4.6.0


glue
An implementation of interpreted string literals, inspired by Python’s Literal String Interpolation https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0498/ and Docstrings https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0257/ and Julia’s Triple-Quoted String Literals https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1.3/manual/strings/#Triple-Quoted-String-Literals-1.
1.8.1


rlang
A toolbox for working with base types, core R features like the condition system, and core ‘Tidyverse’ features like tidy evaluation.
1.2.0


lmtest
A collection of tests, data sets, and examples for diagnostic checking in linear regression models. Furthermore, some generic tools for inference in parametric models are provided.
0.9-40


knitr
Provides a general-purpose tool for dynamic report generation in R using Literate Programming techniques.
1.51


psych
A general purpose toolbox developed originally for personality, psychometric theory and experimental psychology. Functions are primarily for multivariate analysis and scale construction using factor analysis, principal component analysis, cluster analysis and reliability analysis, although others provide basic descriptive statistics. Item Response Theory is done using factor analysis of tetrachoric and polychoric correlations. Functions for analyzing data at multiple levels include within and between group statistics, including correlations and factor analysis. Validation and cross validation of scales developed using basic machine learning algorithms are provided, as are functions for simulating and testing particular item and test structures. Several functions serve as a useful front end for structural equation modeling. Graphical displays of path diagrams, including mediation models, factor analysis and structural equation models are created using basic graphics. Some of the functions are written to support a book on psychometric theory as well as publications in personality research. For more information, see the https://personality-project.org/r/ web page.
2.6.3


robustbase
“Essential” Robust Statistics. Tools allowing to analyze data with robust methods. This includes regression methodology including model selections and multivariate statistics where we strive to cover the book “Robust Statistics, Theory and Methods” by ‘Maronna, Martin and Yohai’; Wiley 2006.
0.99-6


texreg
Converts coefficients, standard errors, significance stars, and goodness-of-fit statistics of statistical models into LaTeX tables or HTML tables/MS Word documents or to nicely formatted screen output for the R console for easy model comparison. A list of several models can be combined in a single table. The output is highly customizable. New model types can be easily implemented. Details can be found in Leifeld (2013), JStatSoft doi:10.18637/jss.v055.i08.)
1.39.5


bigutilsr
Utility functions for large-scale data. For now, package ‘bigutilsr’ mainly includes functions for outlier detection and unbiased PCA projection.
0.3.11


graphics
R functions for base graphics.
4.6.0


moments
Functions to calculate: moments, Pearson’s kurtosis, Geary’s kurtosis and skewness; tests related to them (Anscombe-Glynn, D’Agostino, Bonett-Seier).
0.14.1


naniar
Missing values are ubiquitous in data and need to be explored and handled in the initial stages of analysis. ‘naniar’ provides data structures and functions that facilitate the plotting of missing values and examination of imputations. This allows missing data dependencies to be explored with minimal deviation from the common work patterns of ‘ggplot2’ and tidy data. The work is fully discussed at Tierney & Cook (2023) doi:10.18637/jss.v105.i07.
1.1.0


pbapply
A lightweight package that adds progress bar to vectorized R functions (’*apply’). The implementation can easily be added to functions where showing the progress is useful (e.g. bootstrap). The type and style of the progress bar (with percentages or remaining time) can be set through options. Supports several parallel processing backends including mirai and future.
1.7-4


readxl
Import excel files into R. Supports ‘.xls’ via the embedded ‘libxls’ C library https://github.com/libxls/libxls and ‘.xlsx’ via the embedded ‘RapidXML’ C++ library https://rapidxml.sourceforge.net/. Works on Windows, Mac and Linux without external dependencies.
1.4.5


rmarkdown
Convert R Markdown documents into a variety of formats.
2.31


showtext
Making it easy to use various types of fonts (‘TrueType’, ‘OpenType’, Type 1, web fonts, etc.) in R graphs, and supporting most output formats of R graphics including PNG, PDF and SVG. Text glyphs will be converted into polygons or raster images, hence after the plot has been created, it no longer relies on the font files. No external software such as ‘Ghostscript’ is needed to use this package.
0.9-7


utils
R utility functions.
4.6.0


versus
A toolset for interactively exploring the differences between two data frames.
0.3.1


DescTools
A collection of miscellaneous basic statistic functions and convenience wrappers for efficiently describing data. The author’s intention was to create a toolbox, which facilitates the (notoriously time consuming) first descriptive tasks in data analysis, consisting of calculating descriptive statistics, drawing graphical summaries and reporting the results. The package contains furthermore functions to produce documents using MS Word (or PowerPoint) and functions to import data from Excel. Many of the included functions can be found scattered in other packages and other sources written partly by Titans of R. The reason for collecting them here, was primarily to have them consolidated in ONE instead of dozens of packages (which themselves might depend on other packages which are not needed at all), and to provide a common and consistent interface as far as function and arguments naming, NA handling, recycling rules etc. are concerned. Google style guides were used as naming rules (in absence of convincing alternatives). The ‘BigCamelCase’ style was consequently applied to functions borrowed from contributed R packages as well.
0.99.60


doParallel
Provides a parallel backend for the %dopar% function using the parallel package.
1.0.17


doRNG
Provides functions to perform reproducible parallel foreach loops, using independent random streams as generated by L’Ecuyer’s combined multiple-recursive generator [L’Ecuyer (1999), DOI:10.1287/opre.47.1.159]. It enables to easily convert standard ‘%dopar%’ loops into fully reproducible loops, independently of the number of workers, the task scheduling strategy, or the chosen parallel environment and associated foreach backend.
1.8.6.2


ggnewscale
Use multiple fill and colour scales in ‘ggplot2’.
0.5.2


ggrepel
Provides text and label geoms for ‘ggplot2’ that help to avoid overlapping text labels. Labels repel away from each other and away from the data points.
0.9.6


gtools
Functions to assist in R programming, including: - assist in developing, updating, and maintaining R and R packages (‘ask’, ‘checkRVersion’, ‘getDependencies’, ‘keywords’, ‘scat’), - calculate the logit and inverse logit transformations (‘logit’, ‘inv.logit’), - test if a value is missing, empty or contains only NA and NULL values (‘invalid’), - manipulate R’s .Last function (‘addLast’), - define macros (‘defmacro’), - detect odd and even integers (‘odd’, ‘even’), - convert strings containing non-ASCII characters (like single quotes) to plain ASCII (‘ASCIIfy’), - perform a binary search (‘binsearch’), - sort strings containing both numeric and character components (‘mixedsort’), - create a factor variable from the quantiles of a continuous variable (‘quantcut’), - enumerate permutations and combinations (‘combinations’, ‘permutation’), - calculate and convert between fold-change and log-ratio (‘foldchange’, ‘logratio2foldchange’, ‘foldchange2logratio’), - calculate probabilities and generate random numbers from Dirichlet distributions (‘rdirichlet’, ‘ddirichlet’), - apply a function over adjacent subsets of a vector (‘running’), - modify the TCP_NODELAY (‘de-Nagle’) flag for socket objects, - efficient ‘rbind’ of data frames, even if the column names don’t match (‘smartbind’), - generate significance stars from p-values (‘stars.pval’), - convert characters to/from ASCII codes (‘asc’, ‘chr’), - convert character vector to ASCII representation (‘ASCIIfy’), - apply title capitalization rules to a character vector (‘capwords’).
3.9.5


interactions
A suite of functions for conducting and interpreting analysis of statistical interaction in regression models that was formerly part of the ‘jtools’ package. Functionality includes visualization of two- and three-way interactions among continuous and/or categorical variables as well as calculation of “simple slopes” and Johnson-Neyman intervals (see e.g., Bauer & Curran, 2005 doi:10.1207/s15327906mbr4003_5). These capabilities are implemented for generalized linear models in addition to the standard linear regression context.
1.2.0


ISCO08ConveRsions
Implementation of functions to assign corresponding common job prestige scores (SIOPS, ISEI), the official job or group title and the ISCO-88 code to given ISCO-08 codes. ISCO-08 is the latest version of the International Standard Classification of Occupations which is used to organise information on labour and jobs.
0.2.0


patchwork
The ‘ggplot2’ package provides a strong API for sequentially building up a plot, but does not concern itself with composition of multiple plots. ‘patchwork’ is a package that expands the API to allow for arbitrarily complex composition of plots by, among others, providing mathematical operators for combining multiple plots. Other packages that try to address this need (but with a different approach) are ‘gridExtra’ and ‘cowplot’.
1.3.2


pbmcapply
A light-weight package helps you track and visualize the progress of parallel version of vectorized R functions (mcapply). Parallelization (mc.core > 1) works only on nix (Linux, Unix such as macOS) system due to the lack of fork() functionality, which is essential for mc*apply, on Windows.
1.5.1


readr
The goal of ‘readr’ is to provide a fast and friendly way to read rectangular data (like ‘csv’, ‘tsv’, and ‘fwf’). It is designed to flexibly parse many types of data found in the wild, while still cleanly failing when data unexpectedly changes.
2.2.0


scales
Graphical scales map data to aesthetics, and provide methods for automatically determining breaks and labels for axes and legends.
1.4.0


styler
Pretty-prints R code without changing the user’s formatting intent.
1.11.0


textclean
Tools to clean and process text. Tools are geared at checking for substrings that are not optimal for analysis and replacing or removing them (normalizing) with more analysis friendly substrings (see Sproat, Black, Chen, Kumar, Ostendorf, & Richards (2001) doi:10.1006/csla.2001.0169) or extracting them into new variables. For example, emoticons are often used in text but not always easily handled by analysis algorithms. The replace_emoticon() function replaces emoticons with word equivalents.
0.9.3


tidyselect
A backend for the selecting functions of the ‘tidyverse’. It makes it easy to implement select-like functions in your own packages in a way that is consistent with other ‘tidyverse’ interfaces for selection.
1.2.1

title: Environment Setup & Dependencies
Environment Setup & Dependencies
text:
3 Save Functions


Code
# ' @title Save function to file
# ' @description Writes function definition to R script and applies styler
# ' @param fun function object
# ' @param name optional file name (default: function name)
# ' @param dir output directory
# ' @return Invisibly returns NULL
save_function <- function(fun, name = NULL, dir = here::here("assets", "functions")) {
  stopifnot(is.function(fun) && dir.exists(dir))
  fun_name <- if (is.null(name)) deparse(substitute(fun)) else name
  file_path <- file.path(dir, paste0(fun_name, ".R"))
  x <- deparse(fun)
  x[1] <- paste0(fun_name, " <- ", x[1])
  fun_string <- paste0(x, collapse = "\n")
  writeLines(fun_string, con = file_path)
  styler::style_file(file_path)
  return(invisible())
}

# identify all user-defined functions in global environment
.funs <- purrr::keep(ls(), ~ is.function(get(.x)) &&
  identical(environmentName(environment(get(.x))), "R_GlobalEnv"))

# save each function to assets/functions/
purrr::walk(.funs, ~ {
  fun <- get(.x)
  invisible(suppress_output(save_function(fun, name = .x)))
})

title: Data Cleaning & Recoding
text:
Code
# clear global env and force full garbage collection
rm(list = ls(envir = .GlobalEnv), envir = .GlobalEnv)
invisible(gc(full = TRUE))

# detach non-base packages to avoid namespace interference
invisible(lapply(grep(":|autoloads|globalenv", search(),
  invert = TRUE, value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE
), detach, character.only = TRUE, unload = TRUE, force = TRUE))

# source custom functions for data processing and analysis
purrr::walk(file.path(here::here("assets", "functions"), paste0(c(
  "md_to_html", "format_coef", "as_kable", "describe", "sci_to_tex",
  "add_style", "footnote", "order_array", "numeric_df", "update_isco08r",
  "brc14_map", "import_pipes", "update_value_label", "labels_to_string",
  "format_p_value", "set_globals", "isco08_to_jobtitle", "update_brc14",
  "frequency_table", "beautify_names", "summarize_na", "suppress_output"
), ".R")), source)

# set global options and import pipe operators
set_globals()
import_pipes()

# load corrected dataset from step 2 and adjust variable types
main <- haven::read_sav(
  here::here("assets", "data", "saved", "main_corrected.sav"),
  user_na = TRUE
) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(dplyr::starts_with("brc14"), as.integer)) %>%
  dplyr::select(-dplyr::matches("(_cleaned|_init)$|_old"))

title: Data Cleaning & Recoding
text:
1 Select Variables for Analysis
Excludes character-type, derived, and previously-recoded variables; retains identifiers and analytic indicators.


Code
# standardize variable names to lowercase
names(main) %<>% tolower()

# summarize missing values for each variable
missing_values_summary <- summarize_na(main)

# function to determine variable type from character representation
# Update this function in your Quarto file
get_variable_type <- function(x, ...) {
  # Convert haven_labelled to plain numeric or character first
  if (inherits(x, "haven_labelled")) {
    x <- haven::zap_labels(x)
  }
  class(type.convert(as.character(x), as.is = TRUE))
}

# derived variables to exclude from imputation (identifiers, computed composites)
derived_variables <- c(
  "idnummer", "crowding", "hh_comp", "hh_members", "eq_size",
  "income_hh_num", "income_hh_eq", "children_hh_yes"
)

# create dataframe with variable types and exclusion flags
column_types <- stack(sapply(main, get_variable_type))[2:1]
names(column_types) <- c("variable", "character")

# patterns for identifying recoded or classification variables
exclusion_patterns <- paste(
  ".*_(cat|rec)$",
  "^ses_index.*",
  "^isco[2008]+[^r].*",
  "^brc14[_a-z]*",
  "^skill_level",
  sep = "|"
)

# flag variables as character, recoded, or derived
column_types[c("character", "recoded", "derived")] <- lapply(list(
  column_types$character == "character",
  grepl(exclusion_patterns, column_types$variable),
  column_types$variable %in% derived_variables
), as.integer)

# determine which variables to keep (no exclusion flags set)
column_types$keep <- rowSums(column_types[, -1]) == 0

# merge with missing values summary for complete variable info
variable_info <- column_types %>%
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(
    -variable,
    ~ c("\u00b7", "\u00d7")[as.numeric(.) + 1]
  )) %>%
  dplyr::inner_join(missing_values_summary, by = "variable")

# mark occupational classification variables to keep despite recoded flag
variable_info[grep("isco08r|brc14|isei08", variable_info$variable), "keep"] <- "\u00d7"

# sort for display: kept variables first, then by exclusion reason
d <- dplyr::desc
variable_info %<>%
  dplyr::mutate(kd = as.integer(paste0(keep, derived) == "\u00d7\u00d7")) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(d(keep), kd, d(derived), d(recoded)) %>%
  dplyr::select(-kd)

# subset data to keep only selected variables plus identifier
data_cleaned <- main %>%
  subset(select = c(
    "idnummer",
    variable_info$variable[variable_info$keep == "\u00d7"]
  ))




Code
col_names <- c(
  "Variable", "Character", "Recoded", "Derived",
  "Keep", "$\\textit{N}_{missing}$", "$\\%_{missing}$"
)

footnote_text <- glue(
  "$N = {nrow(main)}$; $n = {nrow(variable_info)}$; ",
  "The symbol '\u00d7' indicates that the variable meets the exclusion criteria ",
  "specified in the column header. Variables that were recoded, character type, ",
  "or derived were excluded from imputation."
)

# display exclusion summary table
variable_info %>%
  as_kable(omit_leading_zero = FALSE) %>%
  kableExtra::add_header_above(
    c(" " = 1, "Reasons for Exclusion" = 3, " " = 3)
  ) %>%
  footnote() %>%
  add_style()

title: Data Cleaning & Recoding
text:
2 Recode Variables for Analysis
Transformations enforce consistent directionality, with higher values denoting greater exposure or difficulty.

2.8 Education Level


Code
# education labels for recoded scale
# 0 = no education, 6 = higher professional
education_labels <- c(
  "no education" = 0,
  "primary school" = 1,
  "lower vocational education" = 2,
  "general secondary education" = 3,
  "secondary vocational education" = 4,
  "higher general secondary education" = 5,
  "higher professional education" = 6
)

# recode all education variables to start from 0
data_cleaned <- data_cleaned %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(
      dplyr::matches("^education($|_)"),
      ~ {
        x <- as.numeric(.)
        x[!is.na(x)] <- x[!is.na(x)] - 1
        out <- labelled::labelled(x, education_labels)
        attr(out, "label") <- attr(., "label")
        out
      }
    )
  )

Code
# update BRC14 variable labels from mapping table
update_brc14 <- function(column_name, data, mapping) {
  values <- unique(stats::na.omit(data[[column_name]]))
  labels <- sapply(values, function(x) {
    unique(mapping$BRC2014beroepsgroep_label[
      as.numeric(mapping$BRC2014beroepsgroep) == x
    ])
  })
  values_named <- setNames(values, labels)
  for (i in seq_along(values_named)) {
    eval.parent(substitute(
      update_value_label(
        data,
        column_name,
        as.numeric(values_named[i]),
        names(values_named)[i]
      )
    ))
  }
}

# update ISCO-08 variable labels from job title lookup
update_isco08r <- function(column_name, data) {
  values <- unique(stats::na.omit(data[[column_name]]))
  labels <- sapply(values, function(x) {
    isco08_to_jobtitle(x)
  })
  values_named <- setNames(values, labels)
  for (i in seq_along(values_named)) {
    eval.parent(substitute(
      update_value_label(
        data,
        column_name,
        as.numeric(values_named[i]),
        names(values_named)[i]
      )
    ))
  }
}




Code
# set partner occupation to NA for respondents without partners
data_cleaned$isco08r_partner[data_cleaned$partner == 0] <- NA

# update value labels for ISCO-08 classifications
update_isco08r("isco08r_partner", data_cleaned)
update_value_label(data_cleaned, "isco08r_partner", NA, "No partner")
update_isco08r("isco08r", data_cleaned)
update_isco08r("isco08r_father", data_cleaned)

# update value labels for BRC14 classifications
brc14_map_data <- brc14_map()
update_brc14("brc14", data_cleaned, brc14_map_data)
update_brc14("brc14_mother", data_cleaned, brc14_map_data)
update_brc14("brc14_father", data_cleaned, brc14_map_data)
update_brc14("brc14_partner", data_cleaned, brc14_map_data)

# set partner BRC14 and ISEI-08 to NA for respondents without partners
data_cleaned$brc14_partner[data_cleaned$partner == 0] <- NA
update_value_label(data_cleaned, "brc14_partner", NA, "No partner")
data_cleaned$isei08_partner[data_cleaned$partner == 0] <- NA

title: Data Cleaning & Recoding
text:
3 Assess Missing Data Patterns
Little’s MCAR test diagnoses whether missingness deviates from completely random.


Code
# select variables with non-deterministic missingness for MCAR test
non_deterministic_data <- data_cleaned %>%
  dplyr::select(where(~ sum(is.na(.)) > 0 & sum(is.na(.)) < nrow(data_cleaned)))

if (ncol(non_deterministic_data) > 1) {
  # perform MCAR test with error handling
  mcar_test_result <- tryCatch(
    naniar::mcar_test(non_deterministic_data),
    error = function(e) {
      message("Error in MCAR test: ", e$message)
      return(NULL)
    }
  )
  if (!is.null(mcar_test_result)) {
    mcar_test_result %<>%
      dplyr::mutate(p.value = format_p_value(p.value))
  } else {
    message("MCAR test could not be completed. Review the missing data structure.")
  }
} else {
  message("Insufficient variability in missing data patterns for MCAR test.")
}




Code
col_names <- c("$\\chi^2$", "$df$", "$p$", "Missing Patterns$^{1}$")

footnote_text <- glue(
  "$n={nrow(data_cleaned)}$;",
  "a $p$-value $\\leq$ 0.001 indicates that data are not missing completely at random;",
  "$^{1}$ Represents the number of missing data patterns."
)

mcar_test_result %>%
  as_kable(align = "lllr") %>%
  footnote() %>%

3.1 Exclude Cases with Critical Missing Data


Code
# identify cases missing both outcome and key predictor
cases_with_missing_dhd_values <- which(
  is.na(data_cleaned$dhd_index) & is.na(data_cleaned$dhd_kcal)
)

percentage_missing <- length(cases_with_missing_dhd_values) / nrow(data_cleaned)

# report exclusion statistics
sentence_missing_dhd <- glue(
  "Out of the total {nrow(data_cleaned)} respondents, ",
  "{length(cases_with_missing_dhd_values)} had ",
  "missing values for both the DHD index and DHD ",
  "kcal ({round(percentage_missing * 100, 2)}%). ",
  "These cases were excluded from further analysis."
)

htmltools::p(sentence_missing_dhd)


Out of the total 1784 respondents,  292 had  missing values for both the DHD index and DHD  kcal (16.37%).  These cases were excluded from further analysis.




3.2 Summarize Remaining Missing Data


Code
# helper function to summarize missingness distribution
summarize_missingness <- function(x) {
  counts <- as.numeric(x)
  props <- paste0(round(as.numeric(names(x)) * 100, 2), "%")
  parts <- paste0(counts[-1], " (", props[-1], ")")
  sentence <- glue(
    "Out of the total {sum(counts)} ",
    "respondents, {counts[1]} had no missing values, ",
    "followed by {paste(parts, collapse = ', ')} ",
    "with increasing proportions of missing values."
  )
  htmltools::HTML(as.character(htmltools::p(sentence)))
}

# subset data excluding cases with missing DHD values
data_subset <- if (length(cases_with_missing_dhd_values) > 0) {
  data_cleaned[-cases_with_missing_dhd_values, ]
} else {
  data_cleaned
}

# define table display parameters
col_names <- c("Variable", rep(c(
  "$n_{\\text{miss}}$",
  "$\\%_{\\text{miss}}$"
), 3))

footnote_text <- glue(
  "Missing values include both system-defined and ",
  "user-defined values, ordered by highest missingness."
)

# summarize missingness stratified by partner status
data_subset %>%
  dplyr::select(-idnummer) %$%
  list(
    No_Partner = dplyr::filter(., partner == 0),
    Partner = dplyr::filter(., partner == 1), .
  ) %>%
  purrr::map2(.y = names(.), ~ summarize_na(.x) %>%
    setNames(c("var", paste0(names(.)[-1], "_", .y)))) %>%
  Reduce(f = function(x, y) dplyr::full_join(x, y, by = "var")) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(var = beautify_names(var)) %>%
  as_kable() %>%
  kableExtra::add_header_above(
    c(" " = 1, "No Partner" = 2, "Partner" = 2, "Total" = 2)
  ) %>%
  footnote() %>%
  add_style()

3.3 Compute Proportional Missingness


Code
# calculate proportion of missing values for key analysis variables
data_subset$prop_miss <- data_subset %>%
  dplyr::select(
    dplyr::matches("_(par|mot|fat)|^dhd|dif|inc|edu|isei", ignore.case = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(-dplyr::matches("brc14|isco08")) %>%
  asplit(1) %>%
  sapply(function(x) mean(is.na(x)))

# tabulate proportions of missing values
prop_miss_table <- table(data_subset$prop_miss)

# display summary of missingness proportions
summarize_missingness(prop_miss_table)


Out of the total 1492  respondents, 250 had no missing values,  followed by 300 (7.69%), 383 (15.38%), 322 (23.08%), 161 (30.77%), 62 (38.46%), 10 (46.15%), 4 (53.85%)  with increasing proportions of missing values.


Code
# determine cutoff for imputation based on missingness proportion (>20%)
# note: structural partner NAs count toward prop_miss, so non-partnered
# respondents tolerate fewer additional missing values than partnered
# respondents before exclusion
cut_off <- min(unique(data_subset$prop_miss[data_subset$prop_miss > 0.2]))

# filter data for imputation, excluding high missingness cases
data_for_imputation <- data_subset %>%
  dplyr::filter(prop_miss < cut_off) %>%
  dplyr::select(-prop_miss)

title: Data Cleaning & Recoding
text:
5 Evaluate SES Composite Scale Validity
Compares composite and domain-specific operationalizations across reliability, factor structure, and discriminant validity criteria.

5.1 Prepare SES Indicators


Code
# extract SES indicators for psychometric evaluation
ses_indicators <- data_for_imputation %>%
  dplyr::select(
    edu_father = education_father,
    edu_mother = education_mother,
    occ_father = isei08_father,
    occ_mother = isei08_mother,
    edu_partner = education_partner,
    occ_partner = isei08_partner,
    partner
  )

# create analysis subsets for different scale configurations
parental_indicators <- ses_indicators %>%
  dplyr::select(edu_father, edu_mother, occ_father, occ_mother)

# partner indicators restricted to partnered respondents
partner_indicators <- ses_indicators %>%
  dplyr::filter(partner == 1) %>%
  dplyr::select(edu_partner, occ_partner)

# combined indicators for full model testing (partnered respondents only)
combined_indicators <- ses_indicators %>%
  dplyr::filter(partner == 1) %>%
  dplyr::select(-partner)




Code
sample_info <- tibble::tibble(
  Subset = c("Total sample", "Partnered respondents", "Parental complete cases"),
  n = c(
    nrow(ses_indicators),
    sum(ses_indicators$partner == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(stats::complete.cases(parental_indicators))
  )
)

footnote_text <- "Sample sizes for validity and reliability analyses."

sample_info %>%
  as_kable() %>%
  footnote() %>%
  add_style()

5.2 Descriptive Statistics of SES Indicators


Code
col_names <- c(
  "Indicator", "$n$", "$n_{miss}$", "$\\%_{miss}$",
  "$\\bar{x}$", "$SD$", "Min", "Max", "Skew", "Kurt", "Domain"
)

footnote_text <- glue(
  "$N = {nrow(parental_indicators)}$; ",
  "Education = educational attainment (ordinal, 0-6); ",
  "Occupation = ISEI-08 occupational status (continuous, 16-90)."
)




Code
# function to compute descriptive statistics for SES indicators
compute_ses_descriptives <- function(data) {
  data %>%
    tidyr::pivot_longer(
      cols = dplyr::everything(),
      names_to = "indicator",
      values_to = "value"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::group_by(indicator) %>%
    dplyr::summarise(
      n = sum(!is.na(value)),
      n_miss = sum(is.na(value)),
      pct_miss = round(100 * mean(is.na(value)), 1),
      mean = round(mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      sd = round(sd(value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      min = min(value, na.rm = TRUE),
      max = max(value, na.rm = TRUE),
      skew = round(moments::skewness(value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      kurt = round(moments::kurtosis(value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      domain = dplyr::case_when(
        grepl("edu", indicator) ~ "Education",
        grepl("occ", indicator) ~ "Occupation",
        TRUE ~ NA_character_
      )
    ) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(domain, indicator)
}

compute_ses_descriptives(parental_indicators) %>%
  as_kable() %>%
  footnote() %>%
  add_style()

5.3 Correlation Structure

Note:   Pearson correlations with pairwise deletion; within-domain correlations appear in diagonal quadrants; cross-domain correlations indicate degree of construct overlap.
















Code
# extract correlation patterns for domain diagnostics
within_edu <- cor_parental["edu_father", "edu_mother"]
within_occ <- cor_parental["occ_father", "occ_mother"]
cross_domain <- mean(c(
  cor_parental["edu_father", "occ_father"],
  cor_parental["edu_father", "occ_mother"],
  cor_parental["edu_mother", "occ_father"],
  cor_parental["edu_mother", "occ_mother"]
))

# determine if cross-domain correlations are substantially lower
cross_lower_than_within <- cross_domain < min(within_edu, within_occ) - 0.10

cor_diagnostics <- tibble::tibble(
  Pattern = c(
    "Within-Education (Father \u2194 Mother)",
    "Within-Occupation (Father \u2194 Mother)",
    "Cross-Domain (Education \u2194 Occupation)"
  ),
  r = round(c(within_edu, within_occ, cross_domain), 3),
  Interpretation = c(
    "Parental homogamy in education",
    "Parental homogamy in occupation",
    dplyr::if_else(cross_lower_than_within,
      "Lower than within-domain (supports distinction)",
      "Comparable to within-domain (suggests overlap)"
    )
  )
)

# conditional footnote based on observed correlation pattern
footnote_text <- dplyr::if_else(
  cross_lower_than_within,
  glue(
    "Cross-domain correlations are substantially lower than within-domain ",
    "correlations, suggesting education and occupation tap distinct constructs."
  ),
  glue(
    "Cross-domain correlations are comparable to or exceed within-domain ",
    "correlations, suggesting substantial construct overlap."
  )
)

cor_diagnostics %>%
  as_kable() %>%
  footnote() %>%
  add_style()

5.4 Reliability Analysis


Code
# complete cases for reliability analysis
parental_complete <- parental_indicators %>%
  tidyr::drop_na()

# cronbach's alpha for parental SES composite (4 indicators)
alpha_parental <- psych::alpha(
  parental_complete,
  check.keys = TRUE,
  warnings = FALSE
)

# mcdonald's omega (hierarchical) with error handling
omega_parental <- tryCatch(
  {
    psych::omega(parental_complete, nfactors = 1, fm = "ml", plot = FALSE)
  },
  error = function(e) NULL
)


Loading required namespace: GPArotation


Omega_h for 1 factor is not meaningful, just omega_t


Warning in schmid(m, nfactors, fm, digits, rotate = rotate, n.obs = n.obs, : Omega_h and Omega_asymptotic are not meaningful with one factor


Warning in cov2cor(t(w) %*% r %*% w): diag(V) had non-positive or NA entries; the non-finite result may be dubious


Code
# spearman-brown for 2-item education scale
parental_edu_only <- parental_indicators %>%
  dplyr::select(edu_father, edu_mother) %>%
  tidyr::drop_na()

r_edu <- cor(parental_edu_only$edu_father, parental_edu_only$edu_mother)
sb_edu <- (2 * r_edu) / (1 + r_edu)

# spearman-brown for 2-item occupation scale
parental_occ_only <- parental_indicators %>%
  dplyr::select(occ_father, occ_mother) %>%
  tidyr::drop_na()

r_occ <- cor(parental_occ_only$occ_father, parental_occ_only$occ_mother)
sb_occ <- (2 * r_occ) / (1 + r_occ)

# partner SES reliability (2 items)
partner_complete <- partner_indicators %>%
  tidyr::drop_na()

r_partner <- cor(partner_complete$edu_partner, partner_complete$occ_partner)
sb_partner <- (2 * r_partner) / (1 + r_partner)




Code
col_names <- c("Statistic", "Value", "Threshold", "Met")

reliability_composite <- data.frame(
  Statistic = c(
    "Cronbach's $\\alpha$ (standardized)",
    "Cronbach's $\\alpha$ (raw)",
    "McDonald's $\\omega$ total",
    "Average inter-item $r$",
    "Complete cases"
  ),
  Value = c(
    round(alpha_parental$total$std.alpha, 3),
    round(alpha_parental$total$raw_alpha, 3),
    if (!is.null(omega_parental)) round(omega_parental$omega.tot, 3) else NA,
    round(alpha_parental$total$average_r, 3),
    nrow(parental_complete)
  ),
  Threshold = c("$\\geq 0.70$", "$\\geq 0.70$", "$\\geq 0.70$", "$\\geq 0.30$", "\u2014"),
  Met = c(
    if (alpha_parental$total$std.alpha >= 0.70) "$\\checkmark$" else "$\\times$",
    if (alpha_parental$total$raw_alpha >= 0.70) "$\\checkmark$" else "$\\times$",
    if (!is.null(omega_parental) && omega_parental$omega.tot >= 0.70) "$\\checkmark$" else "$\\times$",
    if (alpha_parental$total$average_r >= 0.30) "$\\checkmark$" else "$\\times$",
    "\u2014"
  )
)

reliability_composite$Statistic %<>% md_to_html()
reliability_composite$Threshold %<>% md_to_html()
reliability_composite$Met %<>% md_to_html()

footnote_text <- glue(
  "Reliability thresholds based on Nunnally and Bernstein (1994); ",
  "$\\alpha \\geq 0.70$ indicates acceptable internal consistency."
)

reliability_composite %>%
  as_kable() %>%
  footnote() %>%
  add_style()

Code
# function to extract fit indices into standardized format
extract_fit <- function(fit, name) {
  if (is.null(fit)) {
    return(tibble::tibble(
      Model = name, chi_sq = NA, df = NA, p = NA,
      CFI = NA, TLI = NA, RMSEA = NA, SRMR = NA, AIC = NA
    ))
  }
  fm <- lavaan::fitMeasures(
    fit, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "rmsea", "srmr", "aic")
  )
  tibble::tibble(
    Model = name,
    chi_sq = round(fm["chisq"], 2),
    df = fm["df"],
    p = round(fm["pvalue"], 4),
    CFI = round(fm["cfi"], 3),
    TLI = round(fm["tli"], 3),
    RMSEA = round(fm["rmsea"], 3),
    SRMR = round(fm["srmr"], 3),
    AIC = round(fm["aic"], 1)
  )
}

# extract fit indices for model evaluation
fit_m1_cfi <- lavaan::fitMeasures(fit_m1, "cfi")
fit_m1_rmsea <- lavaan::fitMeasures(fit_m1, "rmsea")
fit_m2_cfi <- lavaan::fitMeasures(fit_m2, "cfi")
fit_m2_rmsea <- lavaan::fitMeasures(fit_m2, "rmsea")

# define fit acceptability thresholds
cfi_acceptable <- 0.90
rmsea_acceptable <- 0.08

# evaluate model fit against thresholds
m1_fit_acceptable <- fit_m1_cfi >= cfi_acceptable & fit_m1_rmsea <= rmsea_acceptable
m2_fit_acceptable <- fit_m2_cfi >= cfi_acceptable & fit_m2_rmsea <= rmsea_acceptable
any_fit_acceptable <- m1_fit_acceptable | m2_fit_acceptable

5.6 Convergent and Discriminant Validity


Code
# function to compute average variance extracted (AVE)
compute_ave <- function(fit, factor_name) {
  loadings <- lavaan::standardizedSolution(fit) %>%
    dplyr::filter(op == "=~", lhs == factor_name) %>%
    dplyr::pull(est.std)
  mean(loadings^2)
}

# function to compute composite reliability (CR)
compute_cr <- function(fit, factor_name) {
  loadings <- lavaan::standardizedSolution(fit) %>%
    dplyr::filter(op == "=~", lhs == factor_name) %>%
    dplyr::pull(est.std)
  sum_l <- sum(loadings)
  sum_e <- sum(1 - loadings^2)
  (sum_l^2) / (sum_l^2 + sum_e)
}

# single-factor model metrics
ave_composite <- compute_ave(fit_m1, "parental_ses")
cr_composite <- compute_cr(fit_m1, "parental_ses")

# two-factor model metrics
ave_edu <- compute_ave(fit_m2, "education_parental")
ave_occ <- compute_ave(fit_m2, "isei08_parental")
cr_edu <- compute_cr(fit_m2, "education_parental")
cr_occ <- compute_cr(fit_m2, "isei08_parental")

# factor correlation from two-factor model
factor_cor <- lavaan::standardizedSolution(fit_m2) %>%
  dplyr::filter(op == "~~", lhs == "education_parental", rhs == "isei08_parental") %>%
  dplyr::pull(est.std)

# fornell-larcker criterion: squared correlation < AVE for each factor
fl_edu <- factor_cor^2 < ave_edu
fl_occ <- factor_cor^2 < ave_occ

# htmt ratio using observed correlations
htmt_num <- mean(c(
  cor_parental["edu_father", "occ_father"],
  cor_parental["edu_father", "occ_mother"],
  cor_parental["edu_mother", "occ_father"],
  cor_parental["edu_mother", "occ_mother"]
))
htmt_denom <- sqrt(
  cor_parental["edu_father", "edu_mother"] *
    cor_parental["occ_father", "occ_mother"]
)
htmt <- htmt_num / htmt_denom

# flag extreme factor correlation (potential model identification issue)
factor_cor_extreme <- abs(factor_cor) > 0.95

    "With only two indicators per factor, discriminant validity tests are unreliable.

}

# comprehensive decision logic incorporating model fit and validity evidence
alpha_ok <- alpha_parental$total$std.alpha >= 0.70
ave_ok <- ave_composite >= 0.50
any_fit_acceptable <- m1_fit_acceptable || m2_fit_acceptable
discriminant_not_established <- !(fl_edu && fl_occ) | factor_cor_extreme

# determine recommendation based on full evidence pattern
if (!any_fit_acceptable) {
  decision <- glue(
    "<div class='callout-important'>
    <strong>Recommendation:</strong> Latent variable modeling is not supported.<br><br>
    Neither measurement model achieves acceptable fit ",
    "(single-factor: CFI = {round(fit_m1_cfi, 3)}, RMSEA = {round(fit_m1_rmsea, 3)}; ",
    "two-factor: CFI = {round(fit_m2_cfi, 3)}, RMSEA = {round(fit_m2_rmsea, 3)}). ",
    "Use observed variable composites:<br>
    <ul>
    <li><code>education_parental</code> = mean of father's and mother's education</li>
    <li><code>isei08_parental</code> = mean of father's and mother's ISEI-08</li>
    </ul>
    </div>"
  )
} else {
  decision <- if (alpha_ok && ave_ok && discriminant_not_established) {
    "Composite SES operationalization is acceptable."
  } else if (fl_edu && fl_occ && !factor_cor_extreme && htmt < 0.85) {
    "Domain-specific operationalization preferred."
  } else {
    "Evidence is mixed."
  }

  decision <- glue(
    "<div class='callout-note'>
    <strong>Recommendation:</strong> {decision}
    </div>"
  )
}

htmltools::HTML(decision)

# naming follows dimension_referent pattern (e.g., education_parental, isei08_parental)
data_for_imputation <- data_for_imputation %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    education_parental = rowMeans(
      dplyr::pick(education_father, education_mother),
      na.rm = FALSE
    ),
    isei08_parental = rowMeans(
      dplyr::pick(isei08_father, isei08_mother),
      na.rm = FALSE
    )
  )

# helper function to update variable labels
update_variable_label <- function(data, column_name, label) {
  labelled::var_label(data[[column_name]]) <- label
  data
}

# apply variable labels for documentation
data_for_imputation <- data_for_imputation %>%
  update_variable_label("education_parental", "Parental Education (mean of father and mother)") %>%
  update_variable_label("isei08_parental", "Parental Occupation (mean ISEI-08 of father and mother)")

# generate summary statistics for parental composites and partner indicators
ses_composite_summary <- data_for_imputation %>%
  dplyr::select(education_parental, isei08_parental, education_partner, isei08_partner) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = dplyr::everything(),
    names_to = "indicator",
    values_to = "value"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(indicator) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    n = sum(!is.na(value)),
    mean = round(mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    sd = round(sd(value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    min = round(min(value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    max = round(max(value, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    definition = dplyr::case_when(
      indicator == "education_parental" ~ "Mean of father's and mother's education (0-6)",
      indicator == "isei08_parental" ~ "Mean of father's and mother's ISEI-08",
      indicator == "education_partner" ~ "Partner's educational attainment (0-6)",
      indicator == "isei08_partner" ~ "Partner's ISEI-08 occupational status",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(grepl("parental", indicator)), indicator)

ses_composite_summary %>%
  as_kable() %>%
  footnote() %>%
  add_style()

Code
# clear global env and force full garbage collection
rm(list = ls(envir = .GlobalEnv), envir = .GlobalEnv)
invisible(gc(full = TRUE))

# detach non-base packages to avoid namespace interference
invisible(lapply(grep(":|autoloads|globalenv", search(),
  invert = TRUE, value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE
), detach, character.only = TRUE, unload = TRUE, force = TRUE))

# source custom functions for data handling and visualization
purrr::walk(file.path(here::here("assets", "functions"), paste0(c(
  "remove_invariant", "md_to_html", "set_globals", "add_style",
  "format_numeric", "as_kable", "format_p_value", "import_pipes",
  "footnote", "beautify_names", "numeric_df", "describe"
), ".R")), source)

# set global options and import pipe operators
set_globals()
import_pipes()

# load dataset prepared for imputation (from step 3)
main <- haven::read_sav(
  here::here("assets", "data", "saved", "data_for_imputation.sav"),
  user_na = FALSE
)
Code
# partner-specific variables with structural missingness (NA by design for non-partnered)
partner_vars <- c("education_partner", "isei08_partner")

# parental SES composites from step 3 (dimension_referent convention)
ses_composites <- c("education_parental", "isei08_parental")

# target variables for univariate outlier detection
univariate_targets <- list(
  dhd_kcal = "iqr",
  dhd_index = "mad"
)

title: Detecting & Removing Outliers
Detecting & Removing Outliers
text:
2 Clean Dataset for Outlier Detection


Code
# remove invariant columns and replace non-finite values with NA
clean_data <- main %>%
  remove_invariant() %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(
      dplyr::where(is.numeric),
      ~ ifelse(is.finite(.x), .x, NA)
    )
  ) %>%
  # select numeric columns with non-zero variance
  dplyr::select(dplyr::where(~ is.numeric(.x) && sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE) != 0))

# identify partner-specific variables present in cleaned data
partner_vars_in_data <- intersect(partner_vars, names(clean_data))

# retain complete cases EXCLUDING partner-specific variables
# this preserves non-partnered respondents with legitimate NA values
clean_data <- if (length(partner_vars_in_data) > 0) {
  clean_data %>%
    dplyr::filter(complete.cases(
      dplyr::select(., -dplyr::any_of(partner_vars_in_data))
    ))
} else {
  clean_data %>%
    dplyr::filter(complete.cases(.))
}

# record sample sizes
n_original <- nrow(main)
n_clean <- nrow(clean_data)
n_excluded_cleaning <- n_original - n_clean




Code
cat(glue(
  "<p><strong>Data Cleaning Summary:</strong> ",
  "Of {n_original} observations in the input dataset, ",
  "{n_clean} ({round(100 * n_clean / n_original, 1)}%) were retained for ",
  "outlier detection after removing cases with missing values on substantive ",
  "variables. Partner-specific variables were excluded from this filter to ",
  "preserve non-partnered respondents.</p>"
))


Data Cleaning Summary: Of 933 observations in the input dataset, 249 (26.7%) were retained for outlier detection after removing cases with missing values on substantive variables. Partner-specific variables were excluded from this filter to preserve non-partnered respondents.

title: Detecting & Removing Outliers
Detecting & Removing Outliers
text:
4 Detect Multivariate Outliers


Code
# detect multivariate outliers using Local Outlier Factor (LOF)
# k is fixed at 20 to avoid the default combined-k behavior of bigutilsr::LOF,
# which aggregates across seq_k = c(4, 10, 30) and complicates interpretation
multivariate_outliers <- function(df, excl = NULL,
                                  id_var = "idnummer", k = 20L) {
  stopifnot(id_var %in% names(df))

  # add row numbers for tracking
  df_with_id <- df %>%
    dplyr::mutate(orig_row = dplyr::row_number())

  # prepare data: remove invariant columns, replace non-finite with NA
  df_clean <- df_with_id %>%
    remove_invariant() %>%
    dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(
      dplyr::where(is.numeric),
      ~ ifelse(is.finite(.x), .x, NA)
    )) %>%
    dplyr::select(dplyr::where(~ is.numeric(.x) && sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE) != 0)) %>%
    dplyr::filter(complete.cases(.))

  df_clean <- numeric_df(df_clean)

  # keep id mapping separate from LOF feature set
  id_map <- df_clean %>%
    dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(c(id_var, "orig_row")))

  # exclude identifiers and specified variables from LOF features
  excl_all <- unique(c(excl, id_var, "orig_row"))
  lof_df <- df_clean %>%
    dplyr::select(-dplyr::any_of(excl_all))

  if (ncol(lof_df) == 0) {
    stop("No numeric variables left for LOF after exclusions.")
  }

  # compute log-LOF with single, explicit k (Breunig et al., 2000).
  # with seq_k of length 1, `combine` is irrelevant because only one score
  # exists per observation; we keep the default `combine = max` for
  # compatibility with bigutilsr's API.
  llof <- bigutilsr::LOF(
    U = as.matrix(lof_df),
    seq_k = k,
    log = TRUE
  )

  # Tukey's adjusted boxplot rule (skew-corrected medcouple correction) is
  # the idiomatic bigutilsr cutoff for LOF output (see `?tukey_mc_up`).
  # Default alpha = 0.05 controls Type-I error after multiple-testing
  # correction; this is the canonical usage in the bigutilsr manual and is
  # retained here rather than tightening to the legacy thresh = 0.001,
  # which corresponds to a percentile rule, not a Type-I error.
  lof_cutoff <- bigutilsr::tukey_mc_up(llof)
  lof_outs_idxs <- which(llof > lof_cutoff)

  # map back to original ids
  out_rows <- id_map$orig_row[lof_outs_idxs]
  out_ids <- id_map[[id_var]][lof_outs_idxs]

  # filter out outliers by id
  df_without_outs <- df %>%
    dplyr::filter(!.data[[id_var]] %in% out_ids)

  list(
    df = df_without_outs,
    llof = llof,
    cutoff = lof_cutoff,
    k = k,
    id = out_ids,
    row = out_rows,
    n_features = ncol(lof_df),
    features_used = names(lof_df)
  )
}

title: Detecting & Removing Outliers
text:
5 Remove Detected Outliers

5.1 Univariate Outlier Removal


Code
# detect univariate outliers
univariate_analysis <- clean_data %>%
  detect_outliers(
    vars = names(univariate_targets),
    meths = unlist(univariate_targets)
  )

# extract components
uni_summary <- univariate_analysis$summary
uni_results <- univariate_analysis$results
uni_dropped <- univariate_analysis$updated_data %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(outlier))
uni_kept <- univariate_analysis$updated_data %>%
  dplyr::filter(is.na(outlier))




5.2 Multivariate Outlier Removal


Code
# identify columns to exclude from LOF based on variance/uniqueness
cols_low_variance <- names(uni_kept)[sapply(uni_kept, function(x) {
  is.numeric(x) && (length(unique(x)) <= 2 ||
    length(unique(round(x))) > 0.95 * nrow(uni_kept))
})]

# partner-specific variables to exclude (structural missingness)
# uses canonical dimension_referent naming convention
partner_vars_exclude <- c("education_partner", "isei08_partner", "partner")

# combine all exclusions
cols_to_exclude <- unique(c(
  cols_low_variance,
  partner_vars_exclude[partner_vars_exclude %in% names(uni_kept)]
))

# document excluded columns
if (length(cols_to_exclude) > 0) {
  excluded_low_var <- cols_low_variance[cols_low_variance %in% names(uni_kept)]
  excluded_partner <- partner_vars_exclude[partner_vars_exclude %in% names(uni_kept)]

  exclusion_parts <- c()
  if (length(excluded_low_var) > 0) {
    exclusion_parts <- c(exclusion_parts, glue(
      "low variance/near-constant/near-unique: {toString(dQuote(excluded_low_var, q = FALSE))}"
    ))
  }
  if (length(excluded_partner) > 0) {
    exclusion_parts <- c(exclusion_parts, glue(
      "structural missingness (partner-specific): {toString(dQuote(excluded_partner, q = FALSE))}"
    ))
  }

  excluded_msg <- glue(
    "<p><strong>Note.</strong> Columns excluded from multivariate outlier detection:<br>",
    "{paste(paste0('• ', exclusion_parts), collapse = '<br>')}</p>"
  )
} else {
  excluded_msg <- "<p><strong>Note.</strong> No columns were excluded from multivariate outlier detection.</p>"
}
cat(excluded_msg)


Note. Columns excluded from multivariate outlier detection: • low variance/near-constant/near-unique: “idnummer”, “normal_week”, “home_owner”, “woman”, “partner”• structural missingness (partner-specific): “education_partner”, “isei08_partner”, “partner”


Code
# detect and remove multivariate outliers
outliers_removed <- multivariate_outliers(
  df = uni_kept,
  excl = cols_to_exclude
)

lof_scores <- outliers_removed$llof
lof_cutoff <- outliers_removed$cutoff
ids_removed <- outliers_removed$id
n_lof_features <- outliers_removed$n_features

# collect multivariate outliers from original dataset
multi_dropped <- main %>%
  dplyr::filter(idnummer %in% ids_removed) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(outlier = "Multivariate")

# combine univariate and multivariate outliers
cases_with_outliers <- dplyr::bind_rows(uni_dropped, multi_dropped) %>%
  dplyr::distinct(idnummer, .keep_all = TRUE)

# calculate removal counts
rows_to_remove_uni <- nrow(uni_dropped)
rows_to_remove_multi <- nrow(multi_dropped)
expected_removed <- rows_to_remove_uni + rows_to_remove_multi
actual_removed <- nrow(cases_with_outliers)

# check for overlap
overlap_count <- expected_removed - actual_removed
if (overlap_count > 0) {
  cat(glue(
    "<p><strong>Note.</strong> {overlap_count} case(s) were flagged by both ",
    "univariate and multivariate methods and are counted once in the total.</p>"
  ))
}

cat(glue(
  "<p><strong>LOF detection (k = {outliers_removed$k}):</strong> ",
  "{length(ids_removed)} multivariate outlier(s) identified via Tukey's ",
  "adjusted boxplot rule on log-LOF scores (bigutilsr::tukey_mc_up, ",
  "default alpha = 0.05 after multiple-testing correction; ",
  "log-LOF cutoff = {round(lof_cutoff, 3)}; max observed log-LOF = ",
  "{round(max(lof_scores, na.rm = TRUE), 3)}).</p>"
))


LOF detection (k = 20): 0 multivariate outlier(s) identified via Tukey’s adjusted boxplot rule on log-LOF scores (bigutilsr::tukey_mc_up, default alpha = 0.05 after multiple-testing correction; log-LOF cutoff = 1.017; max observed log-LOF = 0.548).




Code
cat(glue(
  "<p><strong>LOF Detection Summary:</strong> Multivariate outlier detection ",
  "was performed using {n_lof_features} numeric features after exclusions.</p>"
))


LOF Detection Summary: Multivariate outlier detection was performed using 26 numeric features after exclusions.

text:

)

footnote_text <- glue(
  "Shapiro-Wilk Test$^{{\\dagger}}$;",
  "values are normality test statistics (Shapiro-Wilk $W$ for $n \\leq 5000$, Lilliefors $D$ otherwise); ",
  "smaller $W$ (larger $D$) suggests stronger departure from normality;",
  "'—' indicates variables with $\\leq 4$ unique values (normality test not applicable)"
)




Code
data_without_outliers %>%
  dplyr::select(-idnummer) %>%
  setNames(beautify_names(names(.))) %>%
  describe(
    normality = TRUE,
    print_only = FALSE
  ) %>%
  dplyr::rowwise() %>%
  dplyr::ungroup() %>%
  dplyr::select(-p) %>%
  as_kable() %>%
  footnote() %>%
  add_style()

title: Missing Data Imputation
text:
Code
# clear global env and force full garbage collection
rm(list = ls(envir = .GlobalEnv), envir = .GlobalEnv)
invisible(gc(full = TRUE))

# detach non-base packages to avoid namespace interference
invisible(lapply(grep(":|autoloads|globalenv", search(),
  invert = TRUE, value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE
), detach, character.only = TRUE, unload = TRUE, force = TRUE))

# source custom functions for data handling and analysis
purrr::walk(file.path(here::here("assets", "functions"), paste0(c(
  "set_globals", "summarize_na", "as_kable", "describe", "copy_labels",
  "add_style", "md_to_html", "import_pipes", "footnote", "format_p_value",
  "beautify_names", "row_grouping", "numeric_df"
), ".R")), source)

# set global options and import pipe operators
set_globals()
import_pipes()

# load dataset excluding outliers from step 4
data_without_outliers <- haven::read_sav(
  here::here("assets", "data", "saved", "data_without_outliers.sav")
)

title: Missing Data Imputation
Missing Data Imputation
text:
2 Configure Dataset for Imputation

in: data_without_outliers
out: df_impute (partnered subsample, prepared), df_non_partnered (held out)
rule: filter partner == 1 for imputation; convert columns with <= 5 unique values to factors



Code
# rename variables for clarity and consistency with pipeline conventions
df_with_missings <- data_without_outliers %>%
  dplyr::rename(
    education_self = education,
    isei08_self = isei08
  )

# extract non-partnered data (no partner variables to impute)
df_non_partnered <- df_with_missings %>%
  numeric_df() %>%
  dplyr::filter(partner == 0)

# extract partnered data for imputation
df_partnered <- df_with_missings %>%
  numeric_df() %>%
  dplyr::filter(partner == 1)

# nominal classification codes and derived composites are excluded from
# imputation: codes are not interval-scaled and composites are recomputed
# from imputed components in step 6
passthrough_vars <- grep(
  "^isco08r|^brc14|_parental$",
  names(df_partnered),
  value = TRUE
)

# prepare data for missForest: remove identifiers, convert low-cardinality to factors
df_impute <- df_partnered %>%
  dplyr::select(
    -idnummer, -partner,
    -dplyr::all_of(passthrough_vars)
  ) %>%
  haven::zap_labels() %>%
  droplevels() %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(
      dplyr::where(~ dplyr::n_distinct(.x, na.rm = TRUE) <= 5L),
      as.factor
    )
  )

# validate column types for missForest compatibility
bad_cols <- names(df_impute)[!(vapply(df_impute, is.numeric, logical(1)) |
  vapply(df_impute, is.factor, logical(1)))]
if (length(bad_cols)) {
  stop(
    "missForest requires numeric or factor columns: ",
    paste(bad_cols, collapse = ", ")
  )
}

# check for completely missing variables (missForest cannot impute these)
all_na <- names(df_impute)[colSums(!is.na(df_impute)) == 0]
if (length(all_na)) {
  stop("Variables with all values missing: ", paste(all_na, collapse = ", "))
}

title: Missing Data Imputation
text:
3 Define Imputation Parameters


Code
# imputation hyperparameters
imputation_params <- list(
  seed = 625L,
  ntree = 200L,
  mtry = floor(sqrt(ncol(df_impute))),
  maxiter = 10L,
  variablewise = TRUE,
  replace = TRUE
)

title: Missing Data Imputation
text:
4 Run missForest Imputation


Code
# set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(imputation_params$seed)

# choose a safe number of workers (reserve one core for system)
n_workers <- max(1L, parallel::detectCores(logical = FALSE) - 1L)

# determine parallelization strategy based on problem size
missing_rate <- vapply(df_impute, function(col) mean(is.na(col)), numeric(1))
n_miss_vars <- sum(missing_rate > 0)

# IMPORTANT: parallelize = "variables" together with variablewise = TRUE
# returns a length-correct but all-zero OOBerror vector in current missForest
# (the imputation itself completes correctly but OOB diagnostics are useless).
# Serial execution preserves per-variable OOB reporting. Runtime impact is
# modest for this dataset (911 rows, ~31 variables, 200 trees): tens of
# seconds to about a minute on a modern laptop.
parallelize <- "no"

# detect missForest capabilities (legacy randomForest vs ranger backend)
mf_formals <- names(formals(missForest::missForest))
has_backend <- "backend" %in% mf_formals
has_num_threads <- "num.threads" %in% mf_formals

# do not pass a backend argument; let missForest use its default.
# Earlier attempts to explicitly set backend = "ranger" or to suppress it in
# combination with parallelize = "variables" did not restore OOB reporting;
# the serial parallelize = "no" path above is what fixes it.
backend <- NULL

# set up foreach backend only when missForest needs it
use_foreach <- parallelize == "variables" ||
  (parallelize == "forests" && !has_num_threads)

if (use_foreach) {
  clust <- parallel::makeCluster(n_workers)
  on.exit(parallel::stopCluster(clust), add = TRUE)
  doParallel::registerDoParallel(clust)
  doRNG::registerDoRNG(seed = imputation_params$seed)
}

# assemble missForest arguments
mf_args <- list(
  xmis = df_impute,
  ntree = imputation_params$ntree,
  mtry = imputation_params$mtry,
  maxiter = imputation_params$maxiter,
  variablewise = imputation_params$variablewise,
  verbose = TRUE,
  parallelize = parallelize,
  replace = imputation_params$replace
)

if (has_num_threads && parallelize == "forests") mf_args$num.threads <- n_workers

# execute imputation
imp_mf <- do.call(missForest::missForest, mf_args)

# store results with metadata for documentation
imputed_results <- list(
  imp = imp_mf,
  seed = imputation_params$seed,
  n_workers = n_workers,
  parallelize = parallelize,
  backend = backend,
  ntree = imputation_params$ntree,

saveRDS(

  here::here("assets", "data", "saved", "imputed_results_missforest.rds")

title: Missing Data Imputation
text:
5 Calculate Imputation Performance Metrics


Code
# table metadata for OOB metrics display
footnote_text <- glue(
  "OOB = out-of-bag. Numeric variables report OOB MSE when variablewise = TRUE; ",
  "factor variables report OOB PFC (proportion falsely classified). ",
  "For numeric variables, MSE is rescaled to approximate NRMSE: ",
  "sqrt(MSE) / SD(observed). ",
  "Lower values indicate better OOB performance. ",
  "Settings: ntree = {imputed_results$ntree}, mtry = {imputed_results$mtry}, ",
  "maxiter = {imputed_results$maxiter}, parallelize = {imputed_results$parallelize}."
)

col_names <- c(
  "Variable", "Error Metric", "OOB Imputation Error", "Missing Rate",
  "Rating"
)




Code
# extract imputation results for analysis
imp_mf <- imputed_results$imp

# calculate missing rates for all variables
missing_info <- data.frame(
  var = names(df_impute),
  missing_rate = vapply(df_impute, function(col) mean(is.na(col)), numeric(1)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# compute standard deviations for numeric variables (observed values only)
num_vars <- names(df_impute)[vapply(df_impute, is.numeric, logical(1))]
lookup_sd <- data.frame(
  var = num_vars,
  sd_obs = vapply(df_impute[num_vars], stats::sd, numeric(1), na.rm = TRUE),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# assign variable names to OOB errors
raw_oob <- imp_mf$OOBerror
names(raw_oob) <- colnames(df_impute)

# create base dataframe for variable-wise metrics
mf_metrics <- data.frame(
  var = colnames(df_impute),
  erm = ifelse(vapply(df_impute, is.numeric, logical(1)), "NRMSE (approx)", "PFC"),
  oob = as.numeric(raw_oob),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(lookup_sd, by = "var") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(missing_info, by = "var") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    # for numeric variables, convert OOB MSE to approximate NRMSE
    oob = dplyr::case_when(
      erm == "NRMSE (approx)" & is.finite(sd_obs) & sd_obs > 0 ~ sqrt(oob) / sd_obs,
      erm == "NRMSE (approx)" ~ NA_real_,
      TRUE ~ oob
    ),
    # add a compact rating to prioritize review
    rating = dplyr::case_when(
      missing_rate == 0 ~ "Complete",
      oob < 0.5 ~ "Excellent",
      oob < 0.7 ~ "Good",
      oob < 0.9 ~ "Acceptable",
      TRUE ~ "Review"
    )
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(var, erm, oob, missing_rate, rating)

# compute a global, missingness-weighted summary from variable-wise metrics
global_df <- mf_metrics %>%
  dplyr::filter(missing_rate > 0) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(erm) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    oob = stats::weighted.mean(oob, w = missing_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
    missing_rate = mean(missing_rate),
    rating = "Global (weighted)",
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(var = "Global") %>%
  dplyr::select(var, erm, oob, missing_rate, rating)

# prepare table for display (exclude complete variables)
mf_metrics_tbl <- dplyr::bind_rows(mf_metrics, global_df) %>%
  dplyr::filter(missing_rate > 0 | var == "Global") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(var = beautify_names(var)) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(var == "Global"), erm, var)

# display the metrics table
mf_metrics_tbl %>%
  as_kable(cols = col_names, digits = 3) %>%
  footnote() %>%
  add_style()
